hypotheses.intervention: Remove the 12 regressing skills from the collection (debiasing, regret-minimization, triz, feedback-loops, five-whys-plus, inversion, jobs-to-be-done, leverage-points, lindy-effect, occams-razor, opportunity-cost, systems) — ship only the 22 proven + 2 tie skills.
hypotheses.mechanism: The 12 regressing skills collectively win only 9/36 problems (25% win-rate), dragging the aggregate down by ~11pp relative to the proven 22's ~73% win-rate. Removing negative-signal skills eliminates both the direct losses AND the router-selection risk of firing a harmful skill. The adversarial synthesis identifies these skills as suffering from human-context leakage, missing boundaries, and template-hallucination — failures severe enough that the placebo (neutral filler text) beats the skill. A collection of only proven skills should regress toward the proven-skills mean (~71%) on fresh problems, yielding a +14pp nominal lift with expected shrinkage to ~+10-12pp on a holdout set.
hypotheses.test_design: Generate 200 fresh behavioral problems (balanced across the 22 retained skills, ~9/skill) that were NOT used in run1. Run paired design: full collection (36 skills) vs reduced collection (22 skills) on the SAME problems, same solver (claude-sonnet-4-6), cross-family judge (gemini-3.1-pro-preview), blind pairwise. This gives ~180 decisive comparisons (assuming 10% ties), power ≈ 78% for a 10pp lift. Pre-register: H0 = reduced mean ≤ full mean; H1 = reduced mean > full mean + 10pp. Also report per-skill change to confirm no proven skill degrades without its overlap-cluster sibling. Critical: problems must be new — using run1 problems to both select regressors AND measure lift is circular.
hypotheses.why_it_might_fail: Three failure modes. (1) The n=3/skill classification is noisy — 4 of the 'regressors' at 33% (1/3) may be true-50% skills misclassified by sampling error; removing them discards neutral skills and shrinks the collection for no gain. (2) Some regressors are siblings in overlap clusters with proven skills (inversion←→pre-mortem at 33% vs 100%); the proven skill may depend on the regressor's framing as a contrast anchor — removing it could degrade the proven skill's win-rate. (3) The reduced collection covers fewer problem types, so aggregate win-rate on a DIVERSE holdout set could drop if the removed skills were handling problem subtypes the proven skills don't cover.

hypotheses.intervention: Add a mandatory 'When NOT to Use / Stop Conditions' section to every skill plus strip all human-only stage directions (e.g., 'gather the team,' 'project to age 80,' 'silent brainstorming'). Implement as a harness-level instruction: 'Before applying this thinking skill, check the Stop Conditions section. If any condition matches, do NOT use this skill. Instead, state why and proceed with direct reasoning.'
hypotheses.mechanism: Discrimination (boundaries/when-NOT-to-use) is the weakest rubric dimension at 3.38/5 — a full point below fidelity (4.95). Only ~12/39 skills document anti-patterns. Without stop conditions, skills invite over-application: running a 12-point debiasing audit on a trivial choice, applying systems-thinking ceremony to a one-line fix, interrogating instead of coding. The 6 mislead-flagged skills all exhibit human-context leakage that causes the agent to halt, roleplay, or fabricate. Adding boundaries directly attacks the over-application loss rate. If boundaries prevent even 30% of current losses (14 of 46 losses), the aggregate shifts from 60/106 to 74/106 = 69.8% (+13.3pp nominal). Conservatively assuming half that efficacy (7 fewer losses) plus 2 new losses from boundary-induced false-negatives: +5 net wins → 65/106 = 61.3% (+4.8pp). The upside case: boundaries flip 6 of 12 regressors from ~25% to ~67% as their main flaw (missing stop conditions) is fixed, adding ~15 wins, minus 3 false-negative losses = +12 net → 72/106 = 67.9% (+11.4pp).
hypotheses.test_design: Rewrite all 36 leaf skills with: (a) a '## When NOT to Use' section with ≥3 concrete contra-indications per skill, (b) all human-direction language replaced with agent-appropriate alternatives, (c) a harness preamble instructing the solver to check stop conditions first. Run paired design: old skills vs boundary-augmented skills on 194 fresh problems (the N needed for 80% power at 10pp). Same solver/judge as run1. Stratify problems across skills to ensure per-skill n≥5 for within-skill comparison. Pre-specify: the 6 mislead-flagged skills and the 12 regressors are the primary subgroups; expect disproportionate lift in those 18 skills. Also run the Tier 1 rubric on rewritten skills to confirm discrimination dimension improves from 3.38 to ≥4.0.
hypotheses.why_it_might_fail: Three reasons. (1) Over-application may not be the primary failure mode — the 12 regressors might lose because their CORE REASONING is wrong or inapplicable, not because they lack boundaries. Stop conditions on a fundamentally flawed skill just make it fire less often; they don't make it better when it does fire. A 0/3 skill with boundaries is still a 0/3 skill, just quieter. (2) The harness preamble ('check stop conditions first') adds token overhead that could crowd out useful reasoning in the context window, potentially hurting proven skills that currently work well without it. (3) Stripping human directions may accidentally remove useful analogical scaffolding — 'gather the team' might help the model simulate multi-perspective reasoning even if it can't literally gather humans.

hypotheses.intervention: Bifurcate the collection by trigger-probe result: demote CONCEPTUAL skills (where the 2-sentence trigger ties or beats the full SKILL.md) to lightweight 80-120 word triggers; retain full SKILL.md only for PROCEDURAL skills where the full guide demonstrably beats the trigger. Apply the trigger probe to all 36 skills, classify each, and ship accordingly.
hypotheses.mechanism: The trigger-vs-instruction probe (8 skills tested) shows a clean bifurcation: for CONCEPTUAL skills the model already knows (first-principles, theory-of-constraints), the full 1,500-word guide is dead weight — the trigger ties or beats it. For PROCEDURAL skills (bayesian, debiasing, pre-mortem, opportunity-cost), the full guide earns its length. The convergent contrarian insight from all 3 adversarial reviewers: 'verbose files may be net-negative; durable value is triggers + boundaries, not mental-model tutorials the model already knows.' The mechanism: verbose conceptual files induce 'template-hallucination' — the model fabricates evidence to fill rigid frameworks. A 2-sentence trigger avoids this while preserving the concept-activation. The 12 regressing skills are disproportionately conceptual (debiasing, occams-razor, inversion, systems-thinking, lindy-effect, jobs-to-be-done) — their verbose files may be actively harmful. If demoting 8 regressing conceptual skills to triggers lifts them from ~25% to ~50% (trigger ≥ harmful full guide): +6 wins. If full-guide procedural skills hold at ~73% and proven conceptual skills dip slightly from 73% to 67% on triggers: -2 wins. Net: 60+6-2 = 64/106 = 60.4% (+3.9pp conservative). Upside: if regressing conceptual skills' triggers beat their full guides by more (going to 67%): +10 wins → 68/106 = 64.2% (+7.7pp).
hypotheses.test_design: Phase 1: run the trigger-vs-instruction probe on all 36 skills (currently only 8 tested) at n=5/skill to reliably classify each as CONCEPTUAL (trigger ≥ full) or PROCEDURAL (full > trigger). Phase 2: build two collection variants — (A) full SKILL.md for all 36, (B) triggers for CONCEPTUAL skills + full SKILL.md for PROCEDURAL skills. Run paired behavioral eval on 194 fresh problems with the same solver/judge. Primary metric: aggregate win-rate of B vs A. Secondary: per-skill change stratified by conceptual-vs-procedural classification. Pre-register the classification threshold and the hypothesis that conceptual skills benefit more from demotion than procedural skills lose from it. Also measure token consumption — a secondary benefit hypothesis is that triggers reduce context cost by 60-70%.
hypotheses.why_it_might_fail: Four risks. (1) The trigger probe has only been run on 8 skills at n=3 — the conceptual/procedural classification may be unreliable for the remaining 28 skills, causing us to demote skills that actually benefit from full exposition and retain bloated files for skills that don't. (2) The mechanism assumes regressing skills are 'conceptual' and would improve as triggers, but some regressors (triz, five-whys-plus, opportunity-cost) have substantial procedural content (contradiction matrices, causal chains, explicit tradeoff frameworks) — they may regress for reasons unrelated to verbosity. (3) A 2-sentence trigger may lose the 'authority effect' that makes thinking skills work — the placebo-controlled design already shows that neutral filler performs at 44%, and a bare trigger without the ritual framing may similarly underperform the authoritative full guide. (4) This intervention requires the trigger probe to scale to 36 skills, which is itself a ~$50-100 eval cost at n=5/skill before we even test the main hypothesis.

hypotheses.intervention: Consolidate the 3 unanimous overlap clusters (bayesian+probabilistic → one 'belief-updating' skill; inversion+pre-mortem → one 'surface-failure-paths' skill; model-selection → fold into model-router), sharpen ALL descriptions to be situation-named and ≤200 characters, and add disambiguation guidance to the router for the remaining 2 overlap clusters (root-cause cluster, systems-dynamics cluster).
hypotheses.mechanism: Tier 2 routing shows 99% lenient but only 90% strict-unique accuracy — the 9% gap is entirely the overlap clusters, where the router picks 'whichever fires first' because descriptions don't encode distinctions. When the wrong sibling fires (e.g., five-whys-plus instead of scientific-method), the agent gets a slightly different but not necessarily harmful framework. However, for the inversion↔pre-mortem cluster specifically, the gap is 33% vs 100% win-rate — firing the regressor instead of the proven skill is costly. Merging eliminates this wrong-choice risk entirely. Consolidation also removes conflicting/duplicate instructions when two similar skills both fire. The situation-named description fix (8 skills currently exceed 200 chars) improves the router's ability to match skill to problem. Predicted effect: eliminate the ~3 routing errors that cause measurable harm (wrong-sibling-fires-and-performs-worse), plus ~2 wins from clearer, non-conflicting merged skill instructions. Net: +5 wins → 65/106 = 61.3% (+4.8pp). This is the smallest predicted lift because routing errors are rare (lenient already 99%) and most 'wrong' siblings are still helpful.
hypotheses.test_design: Create merged skills for the 3 unanimous clusters (7 skills → 3) following the existing template but with clear internal variant guidance. Sharpen all descriptions. Run TWO tests: (a) Tier 2 routing accuracy on the same 71-case dataset — predict strict-unique improves from 90% to ≥96%; (b) Tier 3 behavioral on 194 fresh problems comparing old collection (36 skills) vs consolidated collection (32 skills). Use paired design. Primary behavioral metric: aggregate win-rate. Secondary: per-cluster win-rate — specifically test whether the merged inversion+pre-mortem skill outperforms stand-alone inversion (33%) and at least matches stand-alone pre-mortem (100%). This is a stricter test than H1-H3 because it requires a smaller N to detect the routing-mediated lift, but the behavioral signal may be too subtle for the current sample sizes.
hypotheses.why_it_might_fail: Three reasons this could show near-zero lift. (1) The lenient routing is already 99% — the overlap problem barely matters behaviorally because the 'wrong' sibling in a cluster is usually good enough. The 9% strict gap may be a measurement artifact, not a real behavioral cost. (2) Merging distinct skills can create bloated, unfocused 'super-skills' that try to do too much and lose the crispness that made the individual skills work — the merged inversion+pre-mortem skill might perform at 50% (splitting the difference between 33% and 100%) rather than maintaining pre-mortem's 100%. (3) Description length currently doesn't correlate with win-rate (triz at 223 chars → 0%; pre-mortem at 153 chars → 100%), so sharpening descriptions — while good for discoverability — may have zero effect on reasoning quality once the right skill fires.

hypotheses.intervention: Rewrite all 36 skills to address LLM-native failure modes instead of human cognitive biases. Replace human-centric framing ('you tend to anchor on first estimates,' 'your gut reaction,' 'as a leader') with LLM-centric framing ('you may over-weight the user's framing,' 'your training data contains spurious correlations,' 'you lack ground-truth verification'). Add concrete examples of LLM-specific failures (sycophancy, recency bias, calibration overconfidence, template-hallucination) to each skill's examples section.
hypotheses.mechanism: The adversarial synthesis's #1 finding (C1, 3/3 reviewers unanimous): 'The skills are human artifacts ported to agents without adaptation.' They address how HUMANS fail (System 1 gut feelings, anchoring, social proof, emotional regret) — but LLMs fail differently (sycophancy toward user framing, recency/context-window bias, inability to hold calibrated uncertainty, over-reliance on authoritative-sounding text). A skill that warns about 'emotional decisions' is irrelevant to an LLM; a skill that warns about 'over-weighting the user's last message' is actionable. This mismatch means current skills may be solving the wrong problem — the model applies the framework correctly to a failure mode it doesn't have, producing framework theater without improved reasoning. The 12 regressors are likely the worst offenders: debiasing warns about human cognitive biases irrelevant to LLMs, regret-minimization asks the model to simulate human emotional regret, lindy-effect and jobs-to-be-done are human-market concepts. Rewriting to target LLM-native failures should make skills actionable. If this lifts the 12 regressors from ~25% to ~50%: +9 wins. If proven skills stay at ~73% (they were already partially LLM-relevant): no change. Net: 69/106 = 65.1% (+8.6pp). Conservative: only 6 regressors benefit (+4.5 wins) → 64.5/106 = 60.8% (+4.3pp).
hypotheses.test_design: Phase 1: identify the subset of skills most reliant on human-cognitive-assumptions (Tier 1 rubric already flags 6; extend rubric to score 'LLM-applicability' for all 36). Phase 2: rewrite the bottom-50% on LLM-applicability (predicted ~18 skills). Phase 3: run paired behavioral eval on 194 fresh problems comparing old skills vs LLM-reframed skills. Stratify by pre-rewrite LLM-applicability score — the prediction is that lift concentrates in low-LLM-applicability skills and is near-zero for already-LLM-applicable skills. This tests the mechanism directly. Also run a 'failure-mode alignment' probe: present the solver with problems designed to trigger sycophancy/recency/overconfidence and verify that LLM-reframed skills produce more resistance to these failure modes than human-framed skills.
hypotheses.why_it_might_fail: Three sharp risks. (1) The premise may be wrong: mental models might improve LLM reasoning THROUGH the same mechanisms they improve human reasoning, even if the surface failure modes differ. 'Consider the opposite' (steel-manning) works whether the original bias was emotional or statistical. If the cognitive operations generalize across human and machine cognition, reframing adds no value and may confuse the model by describing failure modes it doesn't recognize in itself. (2) LLMs don't have stable introspection about their own failure modes — telling a model 'you suffer from recency bias' may not help it correct for recency bias any more than telling a human 'you suffer from confirmation bias' helps them overcome it. The skill might need to provide STRUCTURAL correctives (e.g., 're-read the first message in the conversation before concluding') rather than just reframe the diagnosis. (3) The rewrite cost is high (~18 skills × ~1 hour each) and the effect size is uncertain — this is the riskiest hypothesis in both effort and confidence.

hypotheses.intervention: Implement a harness-level 'capability gate': before loading any thinking skill, the router asks 'Can this problem be solved correctly with direct reasoning alone?' If yes (the problem is straightforward coding, fact lookup, or simple logic), skip the skill entirely and answer directly. Only load a thinking skill when the problem matches a specific failure-mode trigger (the problem type that skill's eval problems were designed to catch).
hypotheses.mechanism: The adversarial synthesis notes that the router lacks a 'strong NONE path.' Currently, every problem gets a skill — but many problems don't need one. The Tier 2 negatives test (16 routine requests) shows the router correctly doesn't fire, but the Tier 3 behavioral design intentionally selects problems where naive reasoning FAILS (each problem targets a specific failure mode). In real usage, the skill fires on problems where direct reasoning would have sufficed, adding token overhead and risking framework-theater errors. The meta-problem: skills are tested on their BEST-CASE problems (where naive reasoning fails) but deployed on ALL problems. A capability gate would reduce false-positive skill invocations, especially for the verbose regressing skills. If 30% of Tier 3 problems are 'direct-reasoning-solvable' and would be answered correctly without any skill, and the skill on those problems causes errors (as the regressors do): removing those 32 problems from the skill-condition and assigning them wins improves the aggregate. Specifically: if 32 problems are direct-reasoning-solvable and would be answered correctly (win), but under the skill condition 16 of those become losses (because the verbose skill induces error), the gate recovers ~16 wins. New aggregate: (60+16)/(106) = 71.7% (+15.2pp). This is the most speculative mechanism but has the highest upside because it attacks the over-application problem at the harness level.
hypotheses.test_design: Phase 1: have a frontier judge (gemini-3.1-pro-preview) classify each of the 108 run1 problems as 'skill-required' (naive reasoning would likely fail) vs 'direct-solvable' (straightforward with careful reasoning). Measure: what fraction of current losses occur on direct-solvable problems where the skill induced error? Phase 2: implement the gate as a harness pre-check. Phase 3: run paired eval on 194 fresh problems where half are direct-solvable and half are skill-required. Compare gated collection vs ungated collection. The key prediction: gating improves win-rate on direct-solvable problems (by preventing over-application) without hurting win-rate on skill-required problems. If the gated collection wins more on direct-solvable and ties on skill-required, the mechanism is confirmed.
hypotheses.why_it_might_fail: The steepest risk: the entire premise that skills HARM on easy problems may be false. The placebo-controlled design already shows that neutral filler (placebo) performs at 44% win-rate — worse than skills at 56%. If the placebo is worse than the skill even on 'easy' problems, then the skill isn't causing harm on easy problems; it's just adding token cost. The gate would reduce token cost but not improve win-rate. Second risk: the gate itself may be wrong — classifying a problem as 'direct-solvable' when it actually contains a subtle failure mode, causing the agent to skip a skill it needed. This would ADD losses on skill-required problems, offsetting any gains. Third risk: the gate adds a router call (latency + token cost) that may exceed the savings from skipping skills.

sample_size_view: The current n=108 (106 decisive) is definitively underpowered. Power to detect a 10pp aggregate lift (56%→66%) at α=0.05 two-sided is ~54% — barely better than a coin flip. The Wilson 95% CI on the current 56.5% win-rate is [47.1%, 65.6%], which spans both 'no effect' (50%) and the 66.5% target. None of the per-skill verdicts are statistically valid (binomial exact p≥0.25 even at 3/3). To validate a 10pp lift at 80% power requires ~194 decisive comparisons in a paired same-problem design, or ~373 per arm in a two-independent-group design. The realistic path: generate 200 fresh problems (~6/skill for the targeted subset, or ~10-12/skill for a concentrated per-skill design), use a paired old-vs-new design (same problem, different skill version, blind cross-family judge), and pre-register the hypothesis, skills, and success threshold. At n=200 with a paired design, a true 10pp lift would be detected with ~82% power. The current 108 is enough for a PILOT to rank hypotheses by effect size, but not to confirm any of them — a confirmatory run needs roughly double the dataset.
single_highest_ev_bet: H1 (remove the 12 regressing skills). Rationale: (1) It has the cleanest causal mechanism — negative-signal skills drag down the mean; removing them unambiguously removes the drag. (2) It has the narrowest prediction interval because the math is straightforward (proven 22 skills average ~73%, reduced collection should regress toward that mean). (3) It's the cheapest to test — no content creation, no rewrites, just a config change. (4) It's the most falsifiable — if the reduced collection does NOT beat the full collection by ≥8pp on fresh problems, the hypothesis is cleanly rejected and we learn that 'regressing' skills may not be truly harmful (they may just be n=3 false positives). (5) It has the lowest risk of unintended harm — we're removing skills, not modifying them, so proven skills are untouched. (6) It directly informs the other hypotheses: if H1 succeeds, H2-H6 become optional optimizations on an already-strong collection; if H1 fails, it tells us the problem is more subtle than 'bad skills exist' and we must look to H2-H6's more nuanced mechanisms.
